title: Fixing Inconsistent Type Inference for Generic Class with Type Guards

statement.markdown: Implement a generic class called `MathCalculator` that can perform basic arithmetic operations with optional type guards. The class should have methods for addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division. Additionally, it should have a method called `getResult` that returns the result of the last operation. However, there is a logical bug in the implementation that prevents it from working correctly with certain types of operands.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be a string representing the operation to be performed, followed by the operands.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be the result of the operation as a string.
statement.notes: The class should handle both integers and floating-point numbers.

examples.explanation: The result of the addition operation
The result of the subtraction operation
The result of the multiplication operation

hints.text: The issue lies in the way you're handling type guards in the class.

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: The key to solving this problem is to understand how type guards work in TypeScript. A type guard is a function that narrows the type of a value within a specific scope.
editorial.bruteForce: One way to solve this problem is to use a brute-force approach and check the types of the operands manually. However, this approach is inefficient and not scalable.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution is to use the `in` operator to check if the operands are of a specific type and then use type guards to narrow the type of the value.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use the `as` keyword to cast the operands to a specific type and then perform the operation. This approach is more efficient and scalable.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of this approach can be proven by using the `typeof` operator to check the type of the operands and then using type guards to narrow the type of the value.
commonMistakes: Not casting operands to specific types

starterCode.javascript: class MathCalculator<T> { add(a: T, b: T): T { return a + b; } sub(a: T, b: T): T { return a - b; } mul(a: T, b: T): T { return a * b; } div(a: T, b: T): T { return a / b; } getResult(): T { return this._result; } private _result: T; }

buggyStarterCode.javascript: class MathCalculator<T> { add(a: T, b: T): T { return a + b; } sub(a: T, b: T): T { return a - b; } mul(a: T, b: T): T { return a * b; } div(a: T, b: T): T { return a / b; } getResult(): T { return this._result; } private _result: T; constructor(private ops: string, private num1: string, private num2: string) { this._result = this.ops === 'add' ? this.add(this.num1, this.num2) : this.ops === 'sub' ? this.sub(this.num1, this.num2) : this.ops === 'mul' ? this.mul(this.num1, this.num2) : this.ops === 'div' ? this.div(this.num1, this.num2) : null; } }
